hook.text: Shine ultraviolet light on metal and electrons fly off. But brighter red light does nothing. Einstein explained why.

buildup.text: Heinrich Hertz first noticed this in 1887. Brighter light should eject more electrons, but color mattered more.

discovery.text: Einstein proposed that light comes in packets—photons. Each photon's energy depends on its frequency, not brightness.

twist.text: This meant light is both a wave and a particle. It was the birth of quantum mechanics.

climax.text: Einstein won his only Nobel Prize in 1921 for this—not for relativity, which was considered too controversial.

punchline.text: Light in packets. Color matters, brightness doesn't. Einstein's Nobel Prize.

quiz.question: Why does red light fail to eject electrons regardless of brightness?
options: Red photons don't have enough energy per photon
Red light doesn't reach the metal surface
Red light is absorbed but not reflected
